body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: partial (TUI message-queueing not verified)
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: gap:mid-turn-injection

## Scenario

mid-turn-message-queued: a slow first prompt keeps the agent busy, and WHILE that turn is still in progress the user types a SECOND message; the agent QUEUES the follow-up and consumes it at the next safe point. User-observable signals (scenario-meanings, mirrored by the claudecode anchor expected.jsonl): the session shows a single CONTIGUOUS working span across the queued-message arrival — NO flicker to ready when the queued input lands and NO flicker to waiting — and the cycle still ends at ready once everything is consumed.

## Agent supports it (agent_supports: partial)

opencode ships an interactive TUI with a live composer, so a user CAN type a second message while a turn is running. Whether that mid-turn message is QUEUED (buffered and submitted at a safe point, folding into one contiguous working span) vs. rejected / immediately interrupting the running turn has NOT been verified against the live TUI — hence partial, not yes. The queuing behavior the scenario locks down is a live-TUI property that has not been observed for opencode.

## daemon observes it (daemon_capability: full)

opencode is the lone `ProcessOwnedStore` adapter (`core/adapters/inbound/agents/opencode/agent.go`): the daemon reads session state straight from the SQLite store via ComputeMetrics, keyed on the `ses_…` session id. A contiguous working span and a clean working→ready close are exactly the read-from-store signals the basic-turn opencode cell already proves the daemon surfaces live. If the agent queues the follow-up (no new turn boundary mid-span), the daemon would render one uninterrupted working span and a single working→ready close — the very arc this scenario asserts. The daemon is NOT the blocker. (The contiguity invariant — no →ready / →waiting flicker as the queued input lands — is the one store-read shape no other opencode cell exercises, so a live recording would be the authority; this is the most likely place an observability_correction would land for this cell once recordable.)

## Why this cell is NOT recordable today (driver gap: mid-turn-injection)

The scenario requires sending a SECOND user message WHILE the first turn is still in progress, so the agent has an in-flight turn to queue against. The opencode interactive driver (`.claude/skills/ir:onboard-agent/scripts/drive-opencode-interactive.sh`) drives opencode through BLOCKING headless `opencode run` subprocesses and implements ONLY three step types in its `case "$TYPE"` dispatch: `send` (launches an `opencode run` subprocess and WAITS for it to complete), `wait_turn` (a no-op — `opencode run` already blocks until the turn ends), and `sleep`. There is NO point at which a message can be injected mid-turn:

1. **`send` blocks to turn completion.** Each `send` shells out to `opencode run` and does not return until that turn has fully completed. By the time the driver could dispatch a second `send`, the first turn is already over — there is no in-flight turn to queue against.
2. **Sends run strictly sequentially.** The driver processes script steps one at a time; a second `send` only begins after the prior `opencode run` exits. There is no async / concurrent / queue step type that would deliver a message during the window the first turn is running.

So the queued-message behavior the scenario locks down (a follow-up landing mid-span, buffered into one contiguous working span) cannot be elicited: the driver can only ever produce back-to-back COMPLETED turns, never an in-flight turn with a second message arriving during it.

### Not a false gap (and not caught by recipe-lint)

This is NOT a missing step TYPE that recipe-lint.sh could reject — a two-`send` recipe lints in-grammar (`send|wait_turn|sleep` are all handled). The gap is architectural: headless `opencode run` blocks to turn completion and the driver sends sequentially, so there is no async/queue primitive and no overlapping window in which to inject a mid-turn message. This mirrors the other opencode driver-gap cells (session-reset gap:slash-command, user-esc-interrupt gap:interrupt, model-switch-midsession gap:model-select) whose missing capability is a live-TUI/concurrency primitive recipe-lint likewise cannot catch.

## What would unblock it

Replace or augment `drive-opencode-interactive.sh` with a LIVE-REPL driver (analogous to the tmux-based claudecode/codex/pi/aider drivers): hold the opencode TUI open, send the first (slow) prompt, then — while that turn is still running — type a SECOND message into the composer and submit it, so the agent has an in-flight turn to queue the follow-up against. This requires a held-open live session plus a mid-turn injection step (and a way to verify the TUI actually queues vs. interrupts). Once the driver can inject a message during an in-flight turn, the cell becomes recordable — the daemon already reads session state from the store, so a contiguous working span across the queued arrival and a single working→ready close would be observable.

## Matrix drift

The coverage matrix should record opencode/mid-turn-message-queued as agent_supports:partial (the TUI has a live composer but its mid-turn queuing-vs-interrupt behavior is unverified), driver_capability:gap:mid-turn-injection (headless `opencode run` blocks to turn completion and the driver sends sequentially — no async/queue step and no live-REPL window to inject a second message into an in-flight turn), daemon observability assessed full (ProcessOwnedStore reads the contiguous working span from the SQLite store, same signal basic-turn proves) but untested live for this contiguity shape.

## Confidence

0.8: the driver grammar (`send|wait_turn|sleep`, each `send` a BLOCKING `opencode run`, sequential step processing, no async/queue step) is read directly from the live driver source, so the mid-turn-injection gap is structural and certain. Not higher because (a) opencode's TUI mid-turn QUEUING behavior (queue vs. interrupt) is unverified live — agent_supports is partial, not yes — and (b) the unblocked recording (a follow-up landing mid-span and the daemon rendering one contiguous working span across it) has not been captured; that is the developer task this gap defers.
caveats: Driver-gap, not a daemon limitation: the daemon (ProcessOwnedStore/SQLite) would render a contiguous working span and a clean working→ready close — the same store-read signals basic-turn proves. The blocker is purely the driver's inability to inject a second message during an in-flight turn (gap:mid-turn-injection).

agent_supports is PARTIAL (not yes): opencode's TUI has a live composer, but whether a mid-turn message is QUEUED (buffered, folded into one working span) vs. interrupts/rejects the running turn has NOT been verified against the live TUI — the queuing behavior the scenario asserts is unconfirmed for opencode.

NOT recordable: the scenario needs a SECOND user message injected WHILE a turn is in progress. drive-opencode-interactive.sh drives BLOCKING headless `opencode run` subprocesses (handles only send|wait_turn|sleep) — each `send` waits for the turn to fully complete and sends run strictly sequentially, so there is no in-flight turn to queue against and no async/queue step. Needs a live-REPL opencode driver with a mid-turn injection step; recording deferred — a developer task, out of scope for implement.
recipe-lint.sh does NOT catch this gap: a two-send recipe lints in-grammar; the missing capability is architectural (no async/queue step + blocking `opencode run`), not a missing step TYPE.

sources.note: case "$TYPE" handles only send|wait_turn|sleep; each `send` launches a BLOCKING `opencode run` subprocess and WAITS for completion, and steps run strictly sequentially — no async/queue step and no point at which a message can be injected mid-turn (case dispatch lines 132-159)

opencode is the lone ProcessOwnedStore adapter; the daemon reads session state from the SQLite store via ComputeMetrics keyed on ses_… — a contiguous working span + clean working→ready close WOULD be observable (daemon_capability:full)
sibling proof: the daemon observes the ses_… ready→working→ready arc live from the store — the contiguous-working-span signal this cell asserts is the same store-read shape
anchor: queued_window phase pins the no-→ready / no-→waiting contiguity invariant across the queued arrival, turn_end closes at ready — the observable shape opencode would have once recordable

sibling opencode driver-gap cell — same root cause class: a live-TUI primitive (in-TUI delivery / live held-open session) that the blocking headless `opencode run` driver cannot provide; needs a live-REPL driver
